column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Silicon Germanium Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from https://www.z-beam.com/materials/semiconductor/elemental/silicon-germanium-laser-cleaning

Optimal average power for SiGe surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength for optimal SiGe absorption and minimal substrate damage
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning resolution
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management
Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal on SiGe
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of contaminants

Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

Typical range for semiconductor cleaning to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100W for light contamination on SiGe, max below damage threshold (~500W with short pulses). Source: IPG Photonics specs for Yb-fiber lasers at 1064 nm; studies on Si ablation (similar to SiGe) in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2018).

Effective for contamination removal; slower (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contaminants, faster (1000-2000 mm/s) for light; 500 mm/s optimal for SiGe to balance efficiency and heat control. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Handbook (SPIE, 2020); equipment manuals for scanning galvo systems.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning with minimal HAZ on SiGe; picosecond (ps) for ultra-precision but less common industrially. 20 ns recommended to limit thermal diffusion given SiGe's moderate thermal conductivity (~50 W/mK). Source: Laser physics research in Appl. Phys. A (2019) on pulsed laser ablation of Ge alloys.

Typical for industrial cleaning; 20 kHz balances speed and heat accumulation on reflective SiGe surfaces (absorptivity ~0.4 at 1064 nm); higher rates risk buildup, lower for heavy cleaning. Source: Academic literature in J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. (2021) on pulse train effects in semiconductor processing.

Fluence below SiGe damage threshold (~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm, similar to Si); 1.5 J/cm² safe for contaminant removal without melting (SiGe mp ~1200-1400°C). Source: Ablation threshold studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. (2017) for Si1-xGex alloys.

Smaller for fine surface prep on SiGe wafers (50-200 μm), larger for broad areas; 200 μm recommended for uniform cleaning without excessive overlap heating. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems for micro-cleaning applications.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy; 2 passes typical for SiGe to ensure complete removal while minimizing cumulative damage. Source: Industry case studies in Proc. SPIE (2022) on laser cleaning of semiconductor substrates.

Ensures uniform coverage; 50% optimal for SiGe to avoid gaps or hot spots given beam divergence at 1064 nm. Source: Process optimization studies in Opt. Express (2020) for scanned laser cleaning of alloys.
